title: Documentation home
description: MovenRun documentation: a health-first movement and territory game, its current status, roadmap, technology, and responsible Web3 approach.
keywords: movenrun documentation movenrun turns walking, running, and cycling into a motivating territory game. you move through your real city, nearby zones come to life on the map, and consistency — not extreme performance — is what makes your territory grow. a responsible, optional web3 ownership layer is being explored for later, and is never required to play. in three sentences. movenrun is a health-first movement game that makes your city feel like a playground. today it is a development-stage android beta where the full territory loop runs as an on-device experience, backed by real verification infrastructure and experimental testnet contracts. nothing in movenrun requires a wallet, promises rewards, or represents real-world land. status at a glance current state of the main product layers (last reviewed 12 july 2026) layer status what that means movement sessions (gps, stats, summaries) im

title: Executive summary
description: A short, honest summary of MovenRun: the opportunity, the product, current maturity, and the gates that come before any deeper economy.
keywords: executive summary movenrun exists because staying active is easier when movement is visibly worth something — not financially, but personally: territory that grows, streaks that hold, a city that opens up, and a community that notices. the opportunity most activity apps measure movement; few make people want to move again tomorrow. habit research and a decade of fitness products point at the same gap: numbers alone don't sustain motivation, but visible progress, gentle social pressure, and play do. meanwhile, "move-to-earn" products proved there is appetite for movement games, then collapsed under financial mechanics that punished ordinary users. movenrun takes the motivating part — territory, progress, play — and deliberately leaves the speculative part out. the product movenrun is a mobile movement game. you start a session, walk, run, or cycle, and your route lights up hexagonal zones

title: MovenRun Whitepaper 2026
description: The consolidated MovenRun 2026 whitepaper: problem, product thesis, territory system, technology, responsible Web3 architecture, economic guardrails, roadmap, and risks.
keywords: movenrun whitepaper — 2026 development-stage product document · last reviewed 12 july 2026 · movenrun documentation read this first. movenrun is a development-stage movement and territory product. its core experience does not require a wallet. any future digital assets, tokens, or on-chain ownership features are conditional, may change, and must pass product, security, legal, and community-readiness gates. nothing in these documents is financial advice or a promise of rewards. 1. executive summary movenrun is a health-first movement game that turns a real city into a territory board. walking, running, and cycling light up hexagonal zones along your route; returning keeps them strong; neglect lets them fade. progress is measured in consistency — streaks, districts, collections, clubs — not in speed or payouts. a deliberately optional web3 layer (digital "zone deeds" on base) is being expl

title: Product vision
description: MovenRun's mission, target users, product pillars, and what the product deliberately is not.
keywords: product vision make everyday movement so visibly worthwhile that people look forward to their next walk. mission movenrun helps people build healthier, more consistent movement habits by turning their own city into a game board. the mission is behavioral, not athletic: more people moving more often, at whatever pace fits their lives. target users habit builders — people who want to move more and need the loop to feel rewarding on day 40, not just day 1. city explorers — walkers and cyclists who enjoy discovering streets, parks, and neighborhoods. friendly competitors — people motivated by clubs, streaks, and light territorial rivalry rather than race times. returning movers — people coming back from inactivity who need a game that celebrates showing up. user needs we design for progress that is visible and spatial , not a buried statistic. a reason to return to the same route — and a rea

title: The core loop
description: Move, Capture, Return, Grow — how MovenRun turns everyday movement into visible territory progress, with ownership as an optional deeper layer.
keywords: the core loop move → capture → return → grow. four verbs that turn a walk into territory — with ownership as an optional layer for much later. the loop is closed and complete on its own. the dashed ownership node is optional and future-facing. move a session begins with one tap. walk, run, or cycle — the game treats all three as first-class. foreground gps records the route while live distance, time, and pace stay glanceable. there are no minimum speeds and no failure states for moving slowly. capture your route intersects hexagonal zones; finishing a session brings one of them into your territory with a small celebration. capture is the moment the outing becomes a mark on the map. in the current build this is an on-device simulation ( local preview ) — see territory, capture & defense for exactly what that means. return territory softens with neglect. moving through your zones refre

title: Movement sessions & routines
description: How a MovenRun movement session works today: foreground GPS, live stats, route summaries, saved progress, weekly recaps, and current limitations.
headings: Routines: recaps, objectives, and saved progress
keywords: movement sessions & routines the session flow is the heart of the current beta: start, move, finish, review — then come back tomorrow. implemented in the current mobile build, on-device. starting a session from the today screen, start move opens the session flow: a location-permission gate (first time), a mode context (walk, run, or cycle), then a live session screen. the app uses foreground gps only — tracking runs while the session screen is active, and there are no background location services, geofencing, or push notifications. during the session the route draws live as you move. distance, elapsed time, and pace stay readable at a glance — deliberately simple so attention stays on your surroundings, not the screen. pause and resume are always available. finishing and the route summary finishing produces a summary: distance, duration, pace, the route shape, xp gained, and the terr

title: Territory, capture & defense
description: How zones, capture, defense decay, and fortification work in the current on-device simulation, and what the future multiplayer model adds.
headings: Current implementation vs. the future model
keywords: territory, capture & defense zones make progress spatial. capture makes it yours. decay and defense make it worth returning. local preview — the full system below runs as a single-player, on-device simulation in the current build. what a zone represents a zone is a hexagonal cell of the map — the game's atom of territory. it represents your relationship with a place : visited, held, neglected, defended. it does not represent land, property, or any real-world right, and holding a zone conveys nothing outside the game. (the hexagons come from the h3 geospatial grid — see territory computation for the plain-language explanation.) how routes become territory when a session ends, the route is mapped to the zones it passed through. the summary shows the territory touched, and saving the session captures a zone into your persistent local portfolio, visible on the today screen, your profile,

title: Progression
description: XP, levels, streaks, movement points, collections, season objectives, and district mastery — and how in-app progress differs from transferable assets.
keywords: progression progress in movenrun is in-app, personal, and visible — and deliberately not a currency. implemented on-device in the current build. xp, levels, and streaks xp is earned by finishing sessions and completing objectives; it drives your level. streaks count consecutive active days. the daily rule is generous — one qualifying session keeps the streak alive — because the product optimizes for consistency, not volume. progress awards apply once per local day per goal, an anti-farming rule carried from the first builds. movement points and “locked move” session summaries show movement points . in the current app this counter carries the historical label locked move , and it is important to be precise about what it is: locked move is a display preview, not a token. in the current build it is a value derived from your xp for preview purposes, labeled in-app as “preview · in-app progre

title: Clubs, community & the city layer
description: Clubs, leaderboards, crew missions, districts, rivals, city wars, and sponsor/event zone previews — what is illustrative today and what becomes multiplayer later.
headings: Districts, city war, and city health
keywords: clubs, community & the city layer better habits are easier together. clubs and the city layer make the map feel inhabited — today as clearly-labeled previews, later as real multiplayer. local preview — every system on this page runs on-device with fictional or locally-derived data. there is no real multiplayer, no live leaderboard, no real sponsors or events, and no networked play in the current build. clubs pick one of four local clubs (base builders, riverside runners, campus crew, morning miles). your real local stats power your club's standing on a preview leaderboard, and a club territory dashboard connects your captured zones, districts, and rivals to your club — showing how club play will feel strategically, before it is networked. crew missions a weekly missions board converts your territory, districts, rivals, and objectives into team-style goals. missions are generated loca

title: Zone Deed preview
description: The educational Zone Deed showroom in the current app: what a digital territory record could become, with no wallet, no minting, and no ownership promise.
keywords: zone deed preview an educational showroom for a future idea: territory records you could one day choose to hold on-chain. today it teaches; it does not mint. local preview — the deed showroom screen in the current app. what the showroom is the app includes a read-only deed preview showroom : a gallery that explains, with example deed cards, what a zone deed could represent — a transparent digital record tying a committed player to a zone, with provenance and history. it exists so players can understand the concept long before deciding whether they ever want it. the screen is labeled with its status directly in the ui: local preview · no wallet · no minting . what it is not no wallet — the app contains no wallet connection anywhere. no minting — nothing can be created, claimed, or purchased. no tradability — there is no marketplace, transfer, or price. no ownership promise — viewing the s

title: Wellbeing & accessibility
description: MovenRun's health-first design principles: participation at every fitness level, consistency over intensity, accessibility, and safety-aware feedback.
keywords: wellbeing & accessibility the game is tuned so that a 20-minute walk is a first-class move — because products that only celebrate athletes don't change habits. movement at every level walking, running, and cycling are equal citizens; no mode is “better”. territory strength comes from returning , not from pace or distance records. daily goals are achievable on a lunch break; streaks survive rest-day realities by design intent. there are no public shame mechanics: previews compare you to fictional rivals, not to faster strangers. consistency over extreme performance everything compounding in the game — streaks, district mastery, collections, club contribution — is a function of showing up repeatedly. this is a deliberate inversion of race-oriented apps: movenrun's strongest players are its most regular players. accessibility principles reduced motion respected. the website and docs hon

title: Privacy & movement data
description: What location data MovenRun uses today, what stays on the device, what the backend is designed to receive later, and what is never collected.
headings: What the current app does with location
keywords: privacy & movement data a movement game runs on location — which is exactly why movenrun's default posture is summaries over trails, on-device over uploaded, and labeled over silent. what the current app does with location foreground gps only. location is read while a session screen is active. no background tracking, geofencing, or location-based notifications. on-device state. sessions, zones, xp, streaks, clubs — the current build stores progress locally (asyncstorage). there is no account system and no server sync. summaries, not trails, in derived features. the route-trust history, signal passport, and shareable proof cards intentionally persist and share summary statistics only — no raw gps points, no coordinates, no route paths, no map screenshots. no network submission. the current app does not upload routes to any backend. the verification pipeline exists as separate infrastr

title: Roadmap & release gates

headings: Phase 1 — Health-first movement beta In progress
Phase 2 — Clubs and city challenges Planned

keywords: roadmap & release gates health, trust, and a strong movement loop come first. every later layer is gated by evidence — not by a launch calendar. phases carry exit criteria, not dates . order and content may change as evidence arrives; that flexibility is deliberate and this page is the record of it. four phases; one hard gate. nothing to the right of the gate is promised. phase 1 — health-first movement beta in progress reliable sessions, route summaries, goals, streaks, and territory progress with no wallet required. most of this phase is live in the current on-device beta. exit criteria: the loop feels good over weeks, not sessions; routes map to zones reliably; early retention supports the thesis. phase 2 — clubs and city challenges planned the local previews become real: accounts, verified activity, genuine club membership, local leaderboards, shared territory, and moderated city

description: The MovenRun system map: mobile app, shared package, backend API and workers, database and queue, experimental testnet contracts, and the website — current versus future connections.

title: Mobile application
description: The Expo/React Native app: routing, on-device state, the GPS session architecture, local preview systems, and the APK build pipeline.
keywords: mobile application a single expo/react native app carries the whole current product: the movement loop, the territory previews, and the progression systems — all on-device. implemented — expo sdk 51, react native 0.74, expo router v3, typescript. stack and structure routing : expo router v3 file-based routes — a tab shell (today, clubs, profile) plus ~30 feature screens ( /move/* session flow, /zone/[id] , /territory/* , /route/* trust screens, city-layer screens, /deed-showroom , /network/status ). state : zustand stores persisted to asyncstorage. progress (xp, level, streak, zones, clubs, history) lives on-device; derived screens (recap, objectives, districts, missions) compute on demand so they need no migrations. design system : the daylight cartography tokens live in src/theme.ts (palette, type scale, spacing, radii, shadows, motion). motion uses core animated only — no animation li

title: Territory computation
description: How MovenRun maps routes to zones: hexagonal grids in plain language, why hexagons, and why grid resolution is an implementation detail, not a promise.
keywords: territory computation how a wobbly gps trace becomes crisp territory: hexagons, and one honest rule about them. h3 in plain language movenrun models the world using h3 , an open-source system (originally from uber) that tiles the entire planet into hexagonal cells. every point on earth falls inside exactly one cell at a given resolution, and every cell has a single stable id. that gives the game a shared, deterministic definition of “a zone” — two players standing on the same street corner are always in the same zone. why hexagons? equal neighbors. every hexagon touches six neighbors at the same distance — squares have awkward diagonal neighbors, which distorts adjacency-based gameplay like defense and spread. better fit to movement. routes curve; hexagons approximate circular “reach” around a path better than squares. clean aggregation. cells roll up into coarser cells, which is how zon

title: Backend, routes & verification
description: The route lifecycle: submission, queue processing, validation checks, deduplication, persistence, and the oracle concept — plus honest limits of GPS verification.
keywords: backend, routes & verification the pipeline that will one day decide “this walk really happened” exists today — built, tested, and deliberately not yet serving the app. infrastructure — express + bullmq + drizzle/postgres + redis. not connected to the consumer app. api responsibilities post /gps/submit — accept a route for verification (wallet-signature authenticated, rate-limited). get /gps/verify/:id — report a route's lifecycle status and, when verified, its proof. /zones/* , /battles/* , /users/* — zone-mint signatures, challenge declarations, and user reads for the future ownership layer (testnet-oriented; unused by the current app). route lifecycle submitted → processing → rejected/verified. verification produces a signed proof; using it on-chain is a separate, optional, future step. submit. the route enters a bullmq queue — validation never blocks the http request. validate. t

title: Security & data
description: Wallet-signature authentication, rate limiting, security headers, CORS, data boundaries, and the threat model — including known limitations.
keywords: security & data security work landed before the features that need it — so the pipeline was hardened while it still had zero users. infrastructure — the measures below protect the backend; the current app runs on-device and has no server attack surface of its own. wallet-signature authentication backend write endpoints ( /gps/submit , /zones/mint , /battles/declare ) require proof that the caller controls the wallet address they claim: a signed message checked by middleware, with the authenticated address bound to the request body so nobody can submit routes or declare challenges on someone else's behalf. this matters only for the future opt-in ownership layer — the consumer app has no wallet today. request hardening rate limiting on write endpoints, per address and per ip. security headers via helmet. cors allowlist — only known origins may call the api from browsers. secrets — sign

title: Web3 overview
description: Why Web3 is optional in MovenRun, what stays off-chain, what may move on-chain, and the difference between testnet experiments and any future production layer.
keywords: web3 overview web3 is movenrun's record layer, not its business model — optional by architecture, not by marketing. why optional the core game — sessions, territory, clubs, progression — is complete without any chain. that is a design invariant: if removing web3 would break the product, the product is built wrong. a wallet is never required to move, capture, join clubs, or progress, and no purchase is required to participate. what web3 may add one narrow thing, for committed players who want it: transparent, portable territory records . a zone deed would let a player hold their claim to a zone as an on-chain record with public provenance — useful if you've spent a year making a district yours and want that history to be durable and verifiable. base is the intended chain: low-cost, evm-compatible, consumer-oriented. what stays off-chain all movement data — routes never go on-chain; at mos

title: Zone Deeds
description: The digital territory record concept: the local preview, the experimental V1 testnet contract, the V2 direction under review, and what Zone Deeds are not.
headings: What a Zone Deed is not
keywords: zone deeds a zone deed is a digital territory record: a durable, transparent link between a committed player and a zone. today it exists as an educational preview and a testnet experiment — nothing more. local preview (in-app showroom) · experimental testnet (v1 contract) · under review (v2 design) the concept territory in movenrun is earned by repetition — and for most players, the in-app record is all they will ever want. a zone deed is for the minority who want more durability: an on-chain record (an nft whose id corresponds to the zone's grid cell) carrying ownership history and provenance that no app database controls. think “a plaque for your neighborhood”, not “an asset class”. the deed records a game relationship with a zone. it has no connection to real-world land — the crossed-out link is the point. where it stands today in the app: the deed preview showroom — educational, read

title: Token, economics & governance
description: MovenRun's economic guardrails: in-app progress versus transferable assets, legacy experimental V1 token parameters, governance status, and why nothing here is a financial promise.

keywords: token, economics & governance movenrun's economic design is a set of guardrails plus one legacy experiment — not an offering. this page uses the title “token and economic design” rather than “how to earn” because there is nothing to earn. conditional — everything token-related is subject to redesign, independent audit, legal review, and product-readiness gates. in-app progress vs. transferable assets the distinction every page in these docs maintains in-app progress transferable assets examples xp, streaks, movement points (“locked move” label) tokens, nfts where it lives on your device, in the game would live on-chain exists today? yes no (testnet experiments only, no consumer access) redeemable / tradable? no — explicitly “not a payout” nothing exists to trade; no marketplace is promised “locked move” in the current app is a display preview derived from xp — no balance is stored an

title: Risks & limitations
description: An honest list of MovenRun's risks — GPS spoofing, privacy, density, contract and regulatory risk — and how the roadmap mitigates them.
keywords: risks & limitations a development-stage product earns trust by listing its own weaknesses. here are movenrun's, with the honest mitigation for each. technical risks gps spoofing. location can be faked. current checks (speed, accuracy, duration, hashing, dedup) raise the bar but do not make spoofing impossible. mitigation: nothing of value depends on a route until verification reliability passes its release gate; anti-spoofing hardening (attestation, behavioral signals) is planned work. battery and foreground tracking. sessions use active gps; long sessions cost battery, and backgrounding can interrupt tracking. mitigation: honest ux expectations; background tracking is deliberately not used for privacy reasons. gps accuracy. urban canyons and indoor starts degrade traces. mitigation: the trust preview surfaces quality instead of hiding it; verification tolerances account for it. cont

title: Legal & product disclaimers
description: Development-stage disclaimers: no financial advice, no promise of rewards, no real-world land ownership, and no value promise for testnet assets.
keywords: legal & product disclaimers plain statements, no fine print. these disclaimers apply to the movenrun app, website, and all documentation pages. development-stage product movenrun is in active development. features, availability, design, and timelines may change or be discontinued at any time without notice. preview systems use deterministic or fictional data — including clubs, rivals, leaderboards, sponsors, and events — and do not represent live services, real people, or real organizations. no financial promises nothing in the product or documentation is financial advice, an investment solicitation, or an offer to sell anything. movenrun promises no rewards, earnings, income, yield, or returns of any kind. in-app progress (xp, streaks, movement points, the “locked move” preview counter) is game state — not money, not a token, not redeemable. there is no token sale, no price, and no

title: Frequently asked questions
description: Direct answers: is MovenRun live, is a wallet required, can you earn money, what is Base Sepolia, is V2 deployed, and how movement data is handled.
headings: Can I earn money with MovenRun?
Do Zone Deeds represent real land?
How is my GPS data handled?
How can I follow the project?
keywords: frequently asked questions direct questions, direct answers — with links to the pages that carry the detail. is movenrun live? the marketing website is live, and a development-stage android beta exists (built through an apk pipeline). there is no public app-store release. the full territory experience currently runs as an on-device preview — see current status . is a wallet required? no. the app contains no wallet connection at all. the core experience will never require one — that is an architectural rule, not a temporary state. is movenrun move-to-earn? no. there is no earning mechanic and no token payout. movenrun deliberately avoids tying motivation to token value — that model has repeatedly harmed the users it attracted. movement earns in-app progress: territory, streaks, xp. can i earn money with movenrun? no. nothing in the product is redeemable, tradable, or valuable, and no futu

title: Glossary & references
description: Definitions for MovenRun terms — zones, capture, fortify, Zone Deed, Locked MOVE, testnet, oracle, H3 — plus canonical repository and external references.
headings: Open / incoming pull requests Under review
keywords: glossary & references the terms these docs use, defined once — followed by the sources they are built from. glossary move session a recorded period of walking, running, or cycling: route, distance, duration, pace. zone one hexagonal cell of the map grid — the atom of territory. a digital game concept, never real land. territory the set of zones a player has captured and maintains. capture claiming a zone by finishing a session whose route touched it. currently an on-device simulation. defense a zone's resistance to fading (and, in the future model, to challenges). decays with neglect; refreshed by returning. fortify an explicit action that strengthens a zone you hold. district a named group of zones at city scale, with its own mastery progression. currently fictional groupings. club a team players join for shared motivation and (future) shared territory. currently a local preview wit
